idea.hook: Could you outrun the sunrise on Mercury? 🏃‍♂️🌅
facts: Mercury orbits the Sun in just ~88 Earth days (its year).
It spins on its axis every ~59 Earth days (its sidereal day).
However, due to a unique 3:2 spin-orbit resonance, the time from one sunrise to the next (a solar day) is drastically different.
idea.payoff: From sunrise to sunrise, 176 Earth days pass – that means one solar day on Mercury is exactly TWO Mercurian years!
idea.title: Mercury's CRAZY Long Day! 🤯⏰

scenes.text: Could you outrun the sunrise on Mercury? 🏃‍♂️🌅
scenes.visual: Stylized Mercury slowly rotating against a starry space background. Faint sun in the distance.
Animated Mercury orbiting the Sun quickly. A timer showing '~88 Earth days' appears.

scenes.text: It spins on its axis every ~59 Earth days.
scenes.visual: Animated Mercury spinning on its axis with a rotational arrow. A timer showing '~59 Earth days' appears.

scenes.text: BUT... a unique 3:2 spin-orbit resonance changes everything!
scenes.visual: Mercury shown orbiting the Sun and spinning simultaneously, highlighting the synchronized motion. Subtle 'resonance' lines connect the spin and orbit.

scenes.text: One solar day = 176 Earth days! That's TWO Mercurian years! 🤯
scenes.visual: Extreme close-up of Mercury's barren surface. The Sun slowly crawls across the horizon, emphasizing the incredibly long 'day'. Time counter rapidly ticking up to 176 Earth days.
